3A_Bibliographic.citation: Nagata, J. M., Ganson, K. T., Engel, S., Dooley, E. E., Palar, K., Gooding, H. C., ... & Baker, F. C. (2022). COVID information and masking behaviors in U.S. adolescents: Findings from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study. Preventive Medicine Reports, 29, 101900.

3B_Sample_Data.abcd_release: COVID-19 Rapid Response Research Release (December 2020 survey)
3B_Sample_Data.verbatim_sample_description: We conducted a secondary data analysis of the December 2020 COVID-19 surveys (COVID-19 Rapid Response Research Release). The analytic sample included 4,106 adolescents ages 12-14 years (35% response rate).
3B_Sample_Data.notes: 35% response rate from original ABCD sample.

3C_Exposure.exposure_variable: Frequency of checking COVID-19 information via social media, internet, and television; frequency of parent counseling on mask wearing
3C_Exposure.exposure_instrument: ABCD COVID-19 Rapid Response Research Survey (December 2020)
3C_Exposure.modalities: social media (Facebook, Twitter, Instagram, TikTok, SnapChat), internet (YouTube), television media
3C_Exposure.operationalization: Binary (almost every hour or more vs less) for social media and internet; binary (frequently/very frequently vs less) for parent counseling; ordinal for TV
3C_Exposure.reporter: Self-report (adolescent) for media checking; parent-report for counseling frequency
3C_Exposure.verbatim_exposure_description: Adolescents self-reported their frequency of checking COVID-19 information in the past week via social media, the internet, and television media coverage. Parents also reported the frequency of counseling their children on the importance of wearing a mask.
3C_Exposure.notes: Social media use for COVID information checking, not general social media use.

3D_Outcome.primary_outcome: Always masking behavior (wearing mask all the time in past week)
3D_Outcome.outcome_instrument: ABCD COVID-19 Rapid Response Research Survey self-report: 'I wear a mask over my face or protective gear'
3D_Outcome.base_rate_if_binary: 62.3% reported wearing masks all the time in the past week
3D_Outcome.verbatim_outcome_description: Masking behavior was assessed based on self-reported frequency of wearing a mask or protective gear.
3D_Outcome.notes: Outcome is COVID masking behavior, not a traditional health outcome.

3E_Design_Analysis.model_type_detail: Modified Poisson regression with coefficients transformed to risk ratios, applying ABCD propensity weights
3E_Design_Analysis.covariates_listed: sex, race/ethnicity, household income, parent education, school type, child COVID infection, site
3E_Design_Analysis.verbatim_analysis_description: Modified Poisson regression analyses with coefficients transformed to risk ratios were used to estimate the associations between COVID-19 information and masking behaviors, adjusting for potential confounders including sex, race/ethnicity, household income, parent education, school type, child COVID infection, and study site.

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: Checking COVID-related social media almost hourly or more
3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Adjusted risk ratio for always masking
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: greater frequency of viewing/checking COVID-19 social media (RR 1.09, 95% CI 1.00-1.19) [adjusted]

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: Checking COVID internet news almost hourly or more
3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Adjusted risk ratio for always masking
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Checking COVID news and updates using the internet... almost every hour or more: 0.94 (0.83-1.07) p=0.377 [adjusted]

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: Viewing COVID television media >=1 hour per day
3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Adjusted risk ratio for always masking
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: View COVID television media coverage... an hour per day or more: 1.09 (1.01-1.17) p=0.033 [adjusted]

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: Parent counseling on masking frequently or very frequently
3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Adjusted risk ratio for always masking
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Parent counseling on the importance of wearing a mask: frequently or very frequently: 1.28 (1.20-1.36) p<0.001 [adjusted]

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: Checking COVID-related social media almost hourly or more
3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Unadjusted risk ratio for always masking
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Checking COVID-related social media activity... almost every hour or more: 1.15 (1.07-1.25) p<0.001 [unadjusted]

3G_Reporting_Quality.notes: The adjusted RR for social media checking and always masking is 1.09 (barely significant at p=0.049), but this is not discussed as a small effect. Parent counseling had a notably larger effect (RR 1.28).

3H_Recommendations.recommendation_verbatim: Our findings provide support for the importance of parent counseling and suggest that social media and television may overall support rather than dissuade protective COVID-19 health behaviors in adolescents.

3I_Rhetorical_Moves.discussion_characterization: The discussion frames social media and television exposure to COVID information as generally supportive of protective health behaviors (masking) in adolescents, contrasting with concerns about misinformation. The strongest predictor was parent counseling (RR 1.28). The effect of social media was modest (adjusted RR 1.09, p=0.049) and was attenuated when parent counseling was considered. The discussion is appropriately cautious about the cross-sectional design and social desirability bias.
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.ambiguity_notes: The social media exposure here is specifically about COVID-19 information checking, not general social media use. The mask/protective gear outcome is contextual to the pandemic.
